### Severe Combined Immunodeficiency

- name: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency
- symbol: IL2RG
- chromosome: X
- function: Encodes the common gamma chain subunit of interleukin receptors, essential for immune cell development and function.
- associated_diseases: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID)
- description: SCID, often called 'bubble boy disease,' is a genetic disorder characterized by a profound lack of immune function, leaving individuals highly susceptible to infections. The IL2RG gene mutation is a common cause of X-linked SCID, disrupting the development of T, B, and NK cells. Gene therapy targeting IL2RG is a promising treatment approach being explored in clinical trials.
- sources: [object Object]

### CancerPAM

- name: CancerPAM
- symbol: N/A
- chromosome: N/A
- function: A multi-omics pipeline for identifying patient-specific, tumour-exclusive CRISPR-Cas9 knock-in sites for pro-inflammatory therapy.
- associated_diseases: Cancer
- description: CancerPAM is an advanced computational framework designed to personalize cancer gene therapy. It analyzes a patient's unique tumor genetics to find safe and effective locations for inserting therapeutic genes. This approach aims to enhance the body's immune response to cancer while minimizing off-target effects.
- sources: [object Object]

### Ultra-rare Gene-based Therapy

- name: Ultra-rare Gene-based Therapy
- symbol: N/A
- chromosome: N/A
- function: A network providing funding and resources to advance gene-based therapies for ultra-rare neurological diseases.
- associated_diseases: Ultra-rare Neurological Diseases
- description: The URGenT network focuses on developing and delivering gene therapies for patient populations with extremely rare genetic neurological conditions. It coordinates research and clinical efforts to overcome the unique challenges of treating these diseases. This initiative represents a targeted approach to precision medicine for the most underserved patient groups.
- sources: [object Object]

### N-of-1 Trials

- name: N-of-1 Trials
- symbol: N/A
- chromosome: N/A
- function: A clinical trial design for a single patient, used for individualized, gene-targeted therapies.
- associated_diseases: Rare Genetic Disorders
- description: N-of-1 trials are a research methodology where a single patient is the entire study population, allowing for highly personalized treatment plans. This approach is particularly valuable for ultra-rare diseases where traditional trials are not feasible. It is a key component of the FDA's new pathway for bespoke gene therapies.
- sources: [object Object], [object Object]

### CRISPR-Cas9

- name: CRISPR-Cas9
- symbol: N/A
- chromosome: N/A
- function: A gene-editing tool derived from a bacterial immune system, used for making precise changes to DNA.
- associated_diseases: Various Genetic Diseases
- description: CRISPR-Cas9 is a revolutionary technology that allows scientists to edit parts of the genome by removing, adding, or altering sections of the DNA sequence. It has evolved from its natural role in bacteria into a powerful tool for research and therapy. Its application is central to many current clinical trials for genetic disorders.
- sources: [object Object]

### Gene Therapy

- name: Gene Therapy
- symbol: N/A
- chromosome: N/A
- function: A medical field that uses genes to treat or prevent disease, often by delivering functional genes to replace faulty ones.
- associated_diseases: Rare Diseases, Genetic Disorders
- description: Gene therapy involves modifying the genetic material within a patient's cells to treat or cure disease. It holds immense promise for conditions that currently have no effective treatments, particularly rare genetic disorders. Recent advances have led to its increased use in clinical practice and regulatory approval.
- sources: [object Object]

### CRISPR Screening

- name: CRISPR Screening
- symbol: N/A
- chromosome: N/A
- function: A functional genomics method using CRISPR to systematically knock out or activate genes to identify their function.
- associated_diseases: Cancer, Various Diseases
- description: CRISPR screening is a powerful tool that allows researchers to test the function of thousands of genes at once in a cell. By systematically disabling genes, scientists can identify which ones are critical for cell survival or disease progression. This method is crucial for discovering new therapeutic targets for complex diseases.
- sources: [object Object]

### CRISPR Gene Editing Market

- name: CRISPR Gene Editing Market
- symbol: N/A
- chromosome: N/A
- function: The commercial sector focused on the development and application of CRISPR-based gene editing technologies.
- associated_diseases: Various Diseases
- description: The CRISPR gene editing market is a rapidly growing industry driven by the therapeutic potential of gene editing technologies. It is projected to grow significantly as more treatments move from research to clinical application. This market encompasses companies developing tools, therapies, and supporting infrastructure for gene editing.
- sources: [object Object]

### SGSH (N-sulfoglucosamine sulfohydrolase)

- name: SGSH (N-sulfoglucosamine sulfohydrolase)
- symbol: SGSH
- chromosome: 17q25.3
- function: Lysosomal enzyme that degrades heparan sulfate in the glycosaminoglycan breakdown pathway
- associated_diseases: Sanfilippo Syndrome Type A (MPS IIIA), Mucopolysaccharidosis IIIA
- description: The SGSH gene encodes N-sulfoglucosamine sulfohydrolase, a lysosomal enzyme essential for the stepwise degradation of heparan sulfate, a glycosaminoglycan found in the extracellular matrix. Mutations in SGSH cause Sanfilippo Syndrome Type A (MPS IIIA), a devastating autosomal recessive neurodegenerative disorder that primarily affects children, leading to severe cognitive decline, behavioral disturbances, and premature death, typically in the second decade of life. The gene is located on chromosome 17q25.3. SGSH is the therapeutic target of UX111, an AAV9 gene therapy developed by Ultragenyx that delivers a functional copy of the SGSH gene to restore enzyme activity. Clinical trial data showed that UX111 produces sustained reduction in CSF heparan sulfate levels and stabilizes cognitive decline in treated patients, making SGSH one of the most actively pursued gene therapy targets for pediatric neurodegenerative disease.
- sources: [object Object]

- source_count: 1

### Macular Telangiectasia Type 2 (MacTel)

- name: Macular Telangiectasia Type 2 (MacTel)
- first_seen: 2026-06-22T06:40:00Z
- last_updated: 2026-06-22T06:40:00Z
- source_count: 2
- confidence: high
- description: Macular Telangiectasia Type 2 (MacTel) is a rare bilateral neurodegenerative retinal disorder characterized by progressive deterioration of the macula, leading to gradual central vision loss. The disease involves abnormalities in retinal vasculature and degeneration of photoreceptors in the macular area. Symptoms include blurred vision, difficulty reading, and distortion of central vision. In March 2025, the FDA approved Encelto (revakinagene taroretcel-lwey) as the first-ever treatment for MacTel Type 2, utilizing an encapsulated cell therapy implant that secretes ciliary neurotrophic factor (CNTF) to preserve photoreceptors.

### Sanfilippo Syndrome Type A (MPS IIIA)

- name: Sanfilippo Syndrome Type A (MPS IIIA)
- first_seen: 2026-06-22T06:40:00Z
- last_updated: 2026-06-22T06:40:00Z
- source_count: 2
- confidence: high
- description: Sanfilippo Syndrome Type A (MPS IIIA) is the most severe form of Mucopolysaccharidosis III, a group of rare autosomal recessive lysosomal storage disorders caused by deficiency of the SGSH enzyme. This enzyme is required to break down heparan sulfate; its deficiency leads to toxic accumulation of heparan sulfate in the brain and other tissues. The disease primarily affects the central nervous system, causing severe neurodegeneration with symptoms including developmental delay, behavioral problems, sleep disturbances, progressive cognitive decline, and loss of motor skills. Most patients do survive past their teens. There is currently no approved pharmacological treatment, but UX111 (Ultragenyx), an AAV9 gene therapy, is under FDA review with a resubmitted BLA as of January 2026.

### Zolgensma (AVXS-101)

- name: Zolgensma (AVXS-101)
- category: gene_therapies
- target_gene: SMN1
- disease: 脊髓性肌萎缩症(SMA)
- delivery_method: AAV
- development_stage: approved
- phase: approved
- company: Novartis
- regulatory_status: FDA批准(2019)/EMA批准
- description: AAV9载体递送的SMN1基因替代疗法，全球首个治愈SMA的基因治疗药物，单次静脉注射
- sources: web_search

### Luxturna (voretigene neparvovec)

- name: Luxturna (voretigene neparvovec)
- category: gene_therapies
- target_gene: RPE65
- disease: RPE65突变相关性遗传性视网膜营养不良
- delivery_method: AAV
- development_stage: approved
- phase: approved
- company: Spark Therapeutics/Roche
- regulatory_status: FDA批准(2017)
- description: AAV2载体进行的视网膜下注射基因治疗，治疗由RPE65基因突变引起的遗传性视网膜疾病
- sources: web_search

### Zolgensma Oculengo

- name: Zolgensma Oculengo
- category: gene_therapies
- target_gene: RPE65
- disease: Leber先天性黑蒙10型(LCA10)
- delivery_method: AAV
- development_stage: clinical
- phase: Phase I/II
- company: Novartis

- description: AAV2载体改良版，针对视网膜疾病的局部给药方案
- sources: web_search

### kymriah_001

- genericName: tisagenlecleucel
- tradeName: Kymriah
- chineseName: 基玛利亚
- developer: Novartis (诺华)
- approvalYear: 2017
- approvalAgency: FDA
- drugType: CAR-T cell therapy
- targetAntigen: CD19
- cellOrigin: Autologous T cells
- mechanism: CD19-directed genetically modified autologous T-cell immunotherapy
- indications: B-cell precursor ALL (patients ≤25 years, refractory or 2nd+ relapse), Relapsed/refractory large B-cell lymphoma after 2+ lines of therapy (DLBCL, high grade B-cell lymphoma, DLBCL from FL), Relapsed/refractory follicular lymphoma after 2+ lines of therapy
- efficacy: {"ALL":"83% ORR at 3 months, 80% MRD-negative, 5-year OS 55%, median EFS 43.8 months","DLBCL":"52% ORR, 40% CR, 12% PR, 83% of CR sustained at 12 months","FL":"86% ORR, 68% CR, 85% of CR sustained at 12 months"}
- clinicalTrials: ELIANA, JULIET, ELARA
- majorSideEffects: Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS), Neurological events (ICANS), Cytopenias, Infections
- dosageWeightLE50kg: 0.2-5.0 × 10^6 CAR-positive T cells/kg
- dosageWeightGT50kg: 0.1-2.5 × 10^8 total CAR-positive T cells
- lymphodepletionPreALL: Fludarabine 30mg/m² daily × 4 days + Cyclophosphamide 500mg/m² daily × 2 days
- lymphodepletionPreDLBCL_FL: Fludarabine 25mg/m² daily × 3 days + Cyclophosphamide 250mg/m² daily × 3 days OR Bendamustine 90mg/m² daily × 2 days
- infusionTiming: 2-14 days post lymphodepletion (ALL); 2-11 days (DLBCL); 2-6 days (FL)
- storage: Liquid nitrogen vapor phase cryopreserved
- stability: 36 months when stored properly
- administrationRoute: Intravenous infusion
- specialWarning: Must be administered in medical center equipped to manage CRS and neurotoxicity; premedicate with tocilizumab and corticosteroids as needed
- manufacturer: Novartis Pharmaceuticals Corporation
- stn: 125646
- fdaUrl: https://www.fda.gov/vaccines-blood-biologics/cellular-gene-therapy-products/kymriah
- sourceUrl: https://www.fda.gov/vaccines-blood-biologics/cellular-gene-therapy-products/kymriah

### Verve-102

- name: Verve-102
- company: Verve Therapeutics
- country: 美国
- city: Boston
- founded: 2018
- type: CRISPR基因编辑疗法
- target: PCSK9
- mechanism: 使用CRISPR碱基编辑技术修改肝脏细胞DNA，关闭PCSK9蛋白合成
- indication: 遗传性高胆固醇血症及相关心脑血管疾病
- status: 临床阶段
- stage: Phase 1/2
- lead_candidate: VERVE-101
- lead_mechanism: 体内碱基编辑，单次给药永久降低LDL胆固醇
- lead_status: 全球首个体内碱基编辑疗法完成患者给药
- lead_date: 2022年7月
- pipeline_count: 多个
- ceo_founder: Sekar Kathiresan
- website: https://www.vervetx.com/
- description: Verve Therapeutics是一家专注于开发一次性体内基因编辑疗法的生物技术公司，致力于通过碱基编辑技术治疗心血管疾病。其管线旨在安全地失活肝脏中与CVD风险相关的基因。

### Verve Therapeutics

- name: Verve Therapeutics
- company: Verve Therapeutics, Inc.
- country: 美国
- city: Boston
- founded: 2018
- type: 生物制药公司
- business_model: 体内基因编辑疗法开发
- focus_area: 心血管疾病(CVD)
- technology_platform: 碱基编辑(Base Editing)
- pipeline_focus: 肝脏基因靶点
- key_target_genes: PCSK9
- status: 上市公司
- market_approach: 全球范围
- recent_development: 2025年12月9日发布管线更新
- website: https://www.vervetx.com/
- founder: Sekar Kathiresan
- description: Verve Therapeutics推进单一疗程的体内基因编辑项目，旨在安全地失活导致心血管疾病风险的肝脏基因。公司专注于碱基编辑技术的临床应用转化。

### VERVE-101

- name: VERVE-101
- company: Verve Therapeutics
- country: 美国
- type: 基因编辑药物
- mechanism: 碱基编辑一次修改肝脏DNA
- target_gene: PCSK9
- effect: 永久关闭致病基因
- drug_class:  investigational base editing therapy
- advantage: 单次给药，持久降低LDL胆固醇
- first_patient_dosed: 2022年7月
- significance: 全球首个体内碱基编辑疗法完成患者给药
- disease_area: 高胆固醇血症
- company_statement: Verve创始人Sekar Kathiresan表示VERVE-101是一流的基因编辑药物
- source: 知乎
- source_date: 2022-07-14
- description: VERVE-101是Verve Therapeutics开发的先导基因编辑候选药物，采用碱基编辑技术对肝脏细胞DNA进行一次修改，永久关闭导致高胆固醇的致病基因PCSK9。

### PCSK9

- name: PCSK9
- company: Verve Therapeutics
- country: 美国
- type: 基因靶点
- gene_function: 调控低密度脂蛋白胆固醇代谢
- edit_strategy: 通过基因编辑使PCSK9功能丧失
- therapeutic_goal: 降低血清LDL-C水平
- clinical_benefit: 治疗遗传性高胆固醇及相关心脑血管疾病
- target_tissue: 肝脏
- delivery_method: 体内(in vivo)基因编辑
- technology: CRISPR碱基编辑
- drug_candidate: VERVE-101/VERVE-102
- description: PCSK9是Verve Therapeutics的核心基因编辑靶点。通过碱基编辑技术使PCSK9功能丧失，可有效降低低密度脂蛋白胆固醇(LDL-C)水平，治疗遗传性高胆固醇血症。

- name_en: Luxturna
- generic_name: voretigene neparvovec
- sponsor: Spark Therapeutics/Roche
- approval_date: 2017-12-19

- notes_en: First directly approved gene therapy in US; annual maintenance cost ~$30k per eye

### GT004

- name_en: Skysona
- name_cn: 赛科纳
- generic_name: elivaldogene autotemcel
- sponsor: Bluebird Bio
- approval_date: 2020-12-17
- indication: 脑肾上腺脑白质营养不良 (CALD)
- mechanism: 自体CD34+造血干细胞转导LRP11基因
- type: Ex Vivo Gene Modification
- dosing: 单次静脉输注，经清髓性化疗预处理
- price_usd: 3000000
- regulatory_path: Full Approval with REMS
- notes_en: Lentiviral vector-based gene therapy

## crispr_applications

### CR-mpkpg9e3-6ad71j

- target_genes: Keap1, PHD2, Nrf2, HIF-1α
- target_diseases: diabetic wounds, chronic diabetic wounds
- editing_type: CRISPR_editing
- development_stage: preclinical
- companies: ASGCT, Innovative Genomics Institute, CRISPR Therapeutics
- key_findings: A CRISPR/Cas9-based gene therapy was developed to simultaneously knockdown Keap1 and PHD2, activating the Nrf2 and HIF-1α pathways to combat oxidative stress in diabetic wounds., Delivery was achieved using multifunctional peptide-modified lysine dendrimers (MsRNPs) that showed excellent biocompatibility and efficient gene editing in vitro., A single topical application of the polyplexes in a diabetic mouse model robustly accelerated wound closure, enhanced collagen deposition, and promoted angiogenesis., Delivery was achieved using multifunctional peptide-modified lysine dendrimers (MsRNPs), which showed excellent biocompatibility and efficient gene editing in vitro., A single CRISPR/Cas9 system was engineered to simultaneously knockdown Keap1 and PHD2, leading to synergistic activation of Nrf2 and HIF-1α pathways., This therapy, delivered by peptide dendrimers, reduced ROS levels and robustly accelerated wound closure, enhanced collagen deposition, and promoted angiogenesis in a diabetic mouse model., A single CRISPR/Cas9 system was engineered for the simultaneous knockdown of Keap1 and PHD2, leading to synergistic activation of Nrf2 and HIF-1α pathways., Topical application of the MsRNP-delivered polyplexes in a diabetic mouse model robustly accelerated wound closure, enhanced collagen deposition, and promoted angiogenesis., This study establishes a novel combinatorial gene-editing strategy and a versatile nanoplatform for treating oxidative stress-related pathologies., The EGOLD method was developed for high-throughput detection of CRISPR off-target effects and identification of chromatin context bias., Off-target effects of CRISPR/Cas9 and base editors strongly correlate with open chromatin regions., Incorporating chromatin context data into machine learning models significantly improves off-target prediction accuracy., Genome editing has evolved from early complex methods like ZFNs and TALENs to the dominant, simpler, and more efficient CRISPR-Cas9 system., Advanced variants like base editors and prime editors enable precise single-nucleotide changes and insertions without double-strand breaks, while emerging tools expand manipulation to RNA and epigenetic modifications., Despite challenges like off-target effects and immunogenicity, CRISPR-derived technologies are at the forefront of next-generation genetic medicine, driving progress toward precise and programmable therapies.
- sources: [object Object], [object Object], [object Object], [object Object], [object Object], [object Object], [object Object], [object Object], [object Object], [object Object]
- confidence: {"level":"low","score":15,"source_count":1,"last_validated":"2026-05-25T04:42:37.070Z"}
- first_seen: 2026-05-25T04:27:21.099Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-25T06:26:07.834Z
- vectors: peptide-modified lysine dendrimers (MsRNPs), Adeno-Associated Virus (AAV), lentivirus, adenovirus, lipid nanoparticles, gold nanoparticles, DNA nano clews

### CR-mpkpg9e4-mmdrqv

- target_genes: 
- target_diseases: cancer
- editing_type: CRISPR_editing
- development_stage: preclinical
- companies: 
- key_findings: Lentiviral vectors are being engineered for precise targeting in cancer therapy to overcome limitations of broad tropism and serum sensitivity. Advances include pseudotyping with natural viral envelopes and next-generation retargeting strategies to enhance specificity for immune and tumor cells. Engineered virus-like particles (VLPs) are emerging as precision tools for in vivo delivery of gene editing complexes like CRISPR-Cas9, base-editing, and prime-editing., Lentiviral vectors can be engineered with various pseudotypes and targeting strategies to achieve precise delivery to specific cell types like immune and tumor cells., Next-generation retargeting innovations, including engineered receptor-binding domains and VSV-G mutants, enhance specificity for T cells, B cells, hematopoietic stem cells, and tumor-associated antigens., Engineered virus-like particles (VLPs) are emerging as precision tools for in vivo delivery of CRISPR-Cas9, base-editing, and prime-editing complexes for precision oncology., Lentiviral vectors (LVs) are being engineered for precise targeting in cancer therapy to overcome limitations of broad tropism and serum sensitivity., Advanced pseudotyping strategies using natural and engineered envelopes, along with retargeting innovations, enhance specificity for immune and tumor cells., Engineered virus-like particles (VLPs) are emerging as precision tools for in vivo delivery of CRISPR-based editing complexes for targeted tumor modification and cell-restricted gene editing., Organoids provide physiologically relevant 3D models for biomedical research, addressing limitations of 2D cultures and reducing species-specific discrepancies., Organoids are valuable for propagating hard-to-culture pathogens like human rhinovirus C and norovirus, and for studying viral pathogenesis of SARS-CoV-2 and Zika virus., Organoids have translational utility for antiviral drug screening and preclinical assessment, and can be integrated with technologies like CRISPR gene editing and 3D bioprinting., AI-driven computational models significantly enhance prediction accuracy, algorithmic efficiency, and translational potential in CRISPR-Cas systems for genetic diseases., Deep learning, machine learning, and reinforcement learning approaches are key to these improvements., Ethical challenges, algorithmic bias, and data security remain critical barriers to clinical adoption.
- sources: [object Object], [object Object], [object Object], [object Object], [object Object], [object Object]
- confidence: {"level":"low","score":15,"source_count":1,"last_validated":"2026-05-25T04:42:37.070Z"}
- first_seen: 2026-05-25T04:27:21.100Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-25T04:54:17.046Z
- vectors: lentiviral vectors, VSV-G-pseudotyped LVs, engineered virus-like particles (VLPs)

### CR-mpkpmyef-os0rvq

- target_genes: 
- target_diseases: genetic disorders, oncology
- editing_type: CRISPR_editing
- development_stage: preclinical
- companies: 
- key_findings: Adenoviral vectors are a highly researched gene therapy platform with high transduction efficiency, large transgene capacity, and broad tropism, but their clinical use is limited by immunogenicity, hepatotoxicity, scalability issues, and lack of durable expression., Recent vector engineering innovations, such as rare serotypes, capsid modifications, and third-generation vectors, aim to overcome these challenges., AdVs are being integrated with other technologies like CRISPR-Cas9, nanotechnology, and AI to enhance their precision and adaptability in treating genetic disorders and cancer.
- sources: [object Object]
- confidence: {"level":"low","score":15,"source_count":1,"last_validated":"2026-05-25T04:32:33.444Z"}
- first_seen: 2026-05-25T04:32:33.447Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-25T04:32:33.447Z

### CR-mpkqew9m-9xqkdm

- target_genes: 
- target_diseases: white spot disease, WSD, white spot syndrome virus, WSSV
- editing_type: CRISPR_editing
- development_stage: preclinical
- companies: 
- key_findings: A single nuclei RNA-seq study was performed on the lymphoid organ of Litopenaeus vannamei to understand the impact of WSSV infection., The study constructed the first penaeid shrimp lymphoid organ cell atlas and identified marker genes for different cell types., Multiple genes of interest were identified as potential targets for CRISPR gene editing to develop WSSV-resistant shrimp stocks.
- sources: [object Object]
- confidence: {"level":"low","score":15,"source_count":1,"last_validated":"2026-05-25T04:54:17.046Z"}
- first_seen: 2026-05-25T04:54:17.050Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-25T04:54:17.050Z

### CR-mpkqew9m-5s6sfl

- target_genes: CD133
- target_diseases: Glioblastoma, GBM
- editing_type: CRISPR_editing
- development_stage: preclinical
- companies: 
- key_findings: Autologous CAR-T cells derived from glioblastoma patients show functional deficits and reduced efficacy compared to healthy donor cells in a pre-clinical model., Allogeneic CAR-T cells, generated using CRISPR to knock out the TCR, demonstrate comparable pre-clinical efficacy to autologous models., This work suggests that allogeneic approaches may be a more effective alternative to autologous CAR-T therapy for glioblastoma.
- sources: [object Object]
- confidence: {"level":"low","score":15,"source_count":1,"last_validated":"2026-05-25T04:54:17.046Z"}
- first_seen: 2026-05-25T04:54:17.050Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-25T04:54:17.050Z

### CR-mpkqew9m-kfmtle

- target_genes: CLTA1, HBB, AIFM1, ABL
- target_diseases: 
- editing_type: CRISPR_editing
- development_stage: preclinical
- companies: 
- key_findings: The GoCas12m-FokI chimeric nuclease is a compact gene editor that achieves high-efficiency editing at clinically relevant targets with no detectable off-target activity., Its small size facilitates delivery via adeno-associated virus and other cargo-limited vectors., The system offers a powerful alternative for therapeutic genome editing and a promising tool for in vivo gene therapy applications.
- sources: [object Object]
- confidence: {"level":"low","score":15,"source_count":1,"last_validated":"2026-05-25T04:54:17.046Z"}

### CR-tgida5ygo1

- target_genes: PCSK9
- target_diseases: familial hypercholesterolemia, cardiovascular disease
- editing_type: base_editing
- development_stage: clinical
- companies: Verve Therapeutics, Beam Therapeutics
- key_findings: Verve VERVE-101 uses base editing to inactivate PCSK9 in the liver, showing >80% PCSK9 reduction in Phase Ib trial., Beam Therapeutics BEAM-101 for sickle cell disease uses base editing to reactivate fetal hemoglobin., Base editing avoids double-strand breaks, reducing risk of chromosomal rearrangements compared to standard CRISPR-Cas9.

### CR-yolt101-base-edit

- target_genes: PCSK9
- target_diseases: heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia, HeFH
- editing_type: base_editing
- development_stage: clinical
- companies: YolTech Therapeutics
- key_findings: YOLT-101: first in vivo base editing therapy for HeFH in clinical trial, GalNAc-modified LNP delivery targeting hepatocytes, Phase 1 results: 74.4% PCSK9 reduction and 52.3% LDL-C reduction at 24 weeks (0.6 mg/kg cohort), No grade ≥3 adverse events, Published in Nature Medicine March 2026, Dual-targeting mechanism via ApoE-LDLR and GalNAc-ASGPR pathways

### CR-pm359-prime-edit

- target_genes: NCF1
- target_diseases: p47phox-deficient chronic granulomatous disease, p47-CGD
- editing_type: prime_editing
- development_stage: clinical
- companies: Prime Medicine
- key_findings: PM359: first-ever clinical use of prime editing in humans, Corrects delGT in NCF1 gene using prime editing (no double-strand breaks), Two patients treated: 68% and 91% corrected alleles in colony-forming cells, Near-normal neutrophil NADPH oxidase activity restored within 1 month, Stable for at least 6 months, no serious adverse events, Published in New England Journal of Medicine, Prime Medicine winding down CGD program due to small market size

### Stanford AI-Powered CRISPR Design

- name: Stanford AI-Powered CRISPR Design
- category: crispr_applications
- application_type: AI-enhanced CRISPR design
- description: AI model that accelerates CRISPR guide RNA design and predicts editing outcomes for faster gene therapy development
- key_features: Machine learning predicts gRNA efficiency, Faster gene therapy design cycle, Reduced off-target prediction, Automated optimization
- development_stage: research
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-06-07T22:08:08.574Z
- last_updated: 2026-06-07T22:08:08.574Z

### In Vivo CRISPR Editing 2026 Wave

- name: In Vivo CRISPR Editing 2026 Wave
- category: crispr_applications
- application_type: in vivo gene editing
- description: Major shift from ex vivo to in vivo CRISPR editing with ~250 clinical trials tracked by 2026, using LNP and AAV delivery
- key_features: Approximately 250 CRISPR clinical trials by 2026, Shift toward in vivo delivery approaches, LNP and AAV delivery platforms, Expanding beyond hematological to solid organ diseases
- development_stage: clinical
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-06-07T22:08:08.574Z
- last_updated: 2026-06-07T22:08:08.574Z

### Casgevy

- name: Casgevy
- application: Gene therapy
- target: Sickle cell disease, Beta thalassemia
- crispr_system: CRISPR-Cas9
- status: Approved (US, UK, EU, Switzerland, Canada, Bahrain, Saudi Arabia, UAE)
- description: Casgevy is the first FDA-approved CRISPR-Cas9-based gene therapy. It treats sickle cell disease and beta thalassemia by editing patients' hematopoietic stem cells. The therapy has shown significant clinical success and represents a major milestone in gene editing therapeutics.
- sources: [object Object], [object Object]

### Base Editing

- name: Base Editing
- application: Gene therapy
- target: DNA (small edits)
- crispr_system: Base editing
- status: Clinical trials
- description: Base editing is a CRISPR technology that allows for precise small edits to DNA without double-strand breaks. It is being used in clinical trials to correct specific genetic variants. Researchers are currently dosing patients in trials for various genetic disorders.
- sources: [object Object], [object Object]

### Prime Editing

- name: Prime Editing
- application: Precision genome editing
- target: DNA (precise genetic modifications)
- crispr_system: Prime editing
- status: Research and clinical development
- description: Prime editing is an advanced CRISPR technology that enables precise genetic modifications without inducing double-strand breaks. It allows for a wide range of edits, including small insertions, deletions, and base changes. It is being explored for correcting specific variants in diseases like SCID.
- sources: [object Object], [object Object]

### CRISPR Medicine News Clinical Trials Register

- name: CRISPR Medicine News Clinical Trials Register
- application: Clinical trial tracking
- target: Gene-editing therapeutic candidates
- crispr_system: Various (CRISPR-based)
- status: Active monitoring
- description: This register tracks the advancement of CRISPR-based gene-editing technologies in clinical development. As of February 2025, it monitors approximately 250 clinical trials. Blood disorders, particularly sickle cell disease, lead the field with the majority of Phase 3 trials.
- sources: [object Object]

### SCID Gene Correction

- name: SCID Gene Correction
- application: Gene therapy
- target: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID)
- crispr_system: Base and Prime Editing
- status: Preclinical studies
- description: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing, including base and prime editing technologies, is being used in preclinical studies to correct specific genetic variants causing SCID. These advanced editing approaches aim for precise corrections without the risks associated with traditional methods.
- sources: [object Object]

### Vertex & CRISPR's Gene Editing Therapy

- name: Vertex & CRISPR's Gene Editing Therapy
- application: Gene therapy
- target: Beta Thalassemia
- crispr_system: CRISPR-Cas9
- status: Approved
- description: This therapy, developed by Vertex and CRISPR Therapeutics, is Casgevy, the first approved gene-editing treatment for Beta Thalassemia. It works by editing a patient's own hematopoietic stem cells to produce healthy hemoglobin. Its approval marks a significant advancement for treating rare blood disorders.
- sources: [object Object]

### VERVE-102 In Vivo Base Editing

- name: VERVE-102 In Vivo Base Editing
- application: Cardiovascular disease
- target: PCSK9
- crispr_system: Adenine base editing (ABE)
- status: Clinical trial (Heart-2 study)
- description: VERVE-102, developed by Verve Therapeutics, is an in vivo base editing therapy targeting the PCSK9 gene for the treatment of heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HeFH). Using lipid nanoparticle (LNP) delivery of adenine base editor mRNA and a guide RNA, VERVE-102 introduces a single-base change in PCSK9 that disrupts the gene's function, leading to durable LDL cholesterol reduction. Clinical data showed that one dose of VERVE-102 led to dose-dependent, substantial, and sustained reductions in PCSK9 and LDL cholesterol levels. In October 2025, Eli Lilly acquired Beam Therapeutics' opt-in rights to Verve's base editing cardiovascular programs, highlighting the growing pharmaceutical interest in in vivo gene editing for cardiovascular disease.
- companies: Verve Therapeutics, Beam Therapeutics, Eli Lilly
- sources: [object Object], [object Object]

### FDA Individualized Gene Editing Pathway for Rare Diseases

- name: FDA Individualized Gene Editing Pathway for Rare Diseases
- application: Regulatory framework
- target: Ultra-rare genetic diseases
- crispr_system: CRISPR-Cas9, base editing, prime editing
- status: Active (2025-2026)
- description: The FDA has established a new regulatory pathway designed to accelerate the development of individualized (N-of-1) gene-editing therapies for patients with ultra-rare genetic diseases. This pathway aims to remove regulatory red tape for bespoke therapies that would otherwise be economically unviable to develop through traditional drug approval processes. Under this framework, researchers can develop CRISPR or base editing therapies tailored to a single patient's specific genetic mutation. The pathway includes streamlined requirements for preclinical testing, manufacturing, and clinical trial design. This represents a paradigm shift in regulatory science, acknowledging that conventional drug development pathways are not suited for therapies targeting patient populations as small as one individual. The initiative was highlighted in FDA guidance documents throughout 2025.
- companies: FDA
- sources: [object Object], [object Object]

### VERVE-102 — Base Editing for Hypercholesterolemia

- name: VERVE-102 — Base Editing for Hypercholesterolemia
- target_genes: PCSK9
- target_diseases: Familial Hypercholesterolemia, Atherosclerotic Cardiovascular Disease
- editing_type: base_editing
- development_stage: clinical_trial
- companies: Verve Therapeutics
- mechanism: Adenine base editing (ABE) to introduce a loss-of-function mutation in PCSK9 gene via LNP delivery, permanently lowering LDL cholesterol
- key_findings: First-in-human base editing trial for cardiovascular disease (Heart-2 trial), LNP-mediated liver-targeted delivery of ABE mRNA and sgRNA, Single IV infusion designed to permanently turn off PCSK9, Preclinical data showed >90% PCSK9 editing in non-human primates with sustained LDL reduction, Potential to replace lifelong statin therapy with one-time treatment
- sources: Verve Therapeutics, Nature Medicine, clinicaltrials.gov
- confidence: high
- date_added: 2026-06-25

### Prime Editing for p47phox-Deficient Chronic Granulomatous Disease (PM359)

- name: Prime Editing for p47phox-Deficient Chronic Granulomatous Disease (PM359)
- target_genes: NCF1 (p47phox)
- target_diseases: Chronic Granulomatous Disease (p47phox-deficient
- editing_type: prime_editing
- development_stage: clinical_trial
- companies: Prime Medicine
- mechanism: Prime editing to correct the GT deletion mutation in NCF1 gene, restoring functional p47phox protein in phagocytes
- key_findings: First prime editing therapy to enter clinical trials (IND cleared 2024), Ex vivo editing of autologous hematopoietic stem cells, Preclinical: >50% correction efficiency with no off-target effects detected, Addresses autosomal recessive p47phox-deficient CGD, affecting ~25% of CGD patients
- sources: Prime Medicine, FDA, Nature Biotechnology
- confidence: high
- date_added: 2026-06-25

### BEAM-302 — Base Editing for Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency

- name: BEAM-302 — Base Editing for Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency
- target_genes: SERPINA1
- target_diseases: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency (AATD)
- editing_type: base_editing
- development_stage: clinical_trial
- companies: Beam Therapeutics
- mechanism: Adenine base editing to correct the Z mutation (Glu342Lys) in SERPINA1 gene, restoring normal AAT protein production in liver
- key_findings: BEAM-301/302 program entered clinical development 2024-2025, LNP-delivered base editors targeting liver hepatocytes, Preclinical: 60-70% correction of Z allele in primary human hepatocytes, Potential to address both liver and lung manifestations of AATD
- sources: Beam Therapeutics, Nature, clinicaltrials.gov
- confidence: high
- date_added: 2026-06-25

### CRISPRoff/CRISPRon — Programmable Epigenome Editing

- name: CRISPRoff/CRISPRon — Programmable Epigenome Editing
- target_genes: DNMT3A/3L (methylation writer)
- target_diseases: Various genetic diseases, Cancer, Neurological disorders
- editing_type: epigenome_editing
- development_stage: preclinical
- companies: UCSF, Prime Medicine, Chroma Medicine
- mechanism: Dead Cas9 (dCas9) fused with DNA methyltransferase (CRISPRoff) or demethylation machinery (CRISPRon) to reversibly silence or activate genes without cutting DNA
- key_findings: Published in Cell 2021, with significant therapeutic applications developed through 2025, Can silence most genes in the genome including those not previously targetable, Heritable silencing maintained through cell divisions without continued Cas9 expression, Reversible: CRISPRon can reactivate silenced genes, Therapeutic applications: silencing disease-causing genes (PCSK9, SCNA for Parkinson's)
- sources: Cell, UCSF, Nature Biotechnology
- confidence: high
- date_added: 2026-06-25

### Casgevy Real-World Outcomes — CRISPR for Sickle Cell Disease

- name: Casgevy Real-World Outcomes — CRISPR for Sickle Cell Disease
- target_genes: BCL11A
- target_diseases: Sickle Cell Disease, Beta-Thalassemia
- editing_type: CRISPR_editing
- development_stage: approved
- companies: Vertex Pharmaceuticals, CRISPR Therapeutics
- mechanism: Ex vivo CRISPR-Cas9 editing of autologous hematopoietic stem cells to disrupt BCL11A erythroid enhancer, reactivating fetal hemoglobin (HbF) production
- key_findings: First CRISPR therapy ever approved (FDA Dec 2023, UK Nov 2023), Real-world data 2024-2025: 96.5% of patients free from vaso-occlusive crises for ≥12 months, CLIMB-121 trial: 94% of SCD patients achieved complete response, CLIMB-131 trial: 100% of TDT patients became transfusion-independent, Long-term safety data through 2025 confirms durable HbF expression, Challenge: High cost ($2.2M) and complex manufacturing limit access
- sources: NEJM, FDA, Vertex Pharmaceuticals, ASH 2024
- confidence: high
- date_added: 2026-06-25

## genomic_diagnostics

### Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS)

- name: Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS)
- category: genomic_diagnostics
- technology_type: sequencing
- mechanism: Massively parallel sequencing of DNA fragments
- key_features: High throughput, Cost decreasing rapidly, Multiple platforms available
- applications: Gene panel testing, Whole exome sequencing, Whole genome sequencing, Transcriptomics
- market_size: NGS market projected significant growth 2026-2035
- companies: Illumina, Thermo Fisher, Oxford Nanopore, Pacific Biosciences
- development_stage: clinical
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Liquid Biopsy

- name: Liquid Biopsy
- category: genomic_diagnostics
- technology_type: diagnostics
- mechanism: Detection of circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA), circulating tumor cells, and exosomes in blood
- key_features: Non-invasive, Real-time monitoring, Early detection potential, Minimal sample required
- applications: Cancer early detection, Treatment monitoring, Minimal residual disease, Mutation tracking
- development_stage: clinical
- companies: Guardant Health, Grail (Illumina), Foundation Medicine, Roche
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Whole Genome Sequencing (WGS)

- name: Whole Genome Sequencing (WGS)
- category: genomic_diagnostics
- technology_type: sequencing
- mechanism: Complete sequencing of an organism's entire genome
- key_features: Comprehensive coverage, Non-coding region analysis, Structural variant detection
- applications: Rare disease diagnosis, Cancer genomics, Pharmacogenomics, Population genomics
- development_stage: clinical
- companies: Illumina, Pacific Biosciences, Oxford Nanopore
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Whole Exome Sequencing (WES)

- name: Whole Exome Sequencing (WES)
- category: genomic_diagnostics
- technology_type: sequencing
- mechanism: Sequencing of all protein-coding regions (~1-2% of genome)
- key_features: Cost-effective vs WGS, Captures most disease-causing variants, Clinical utility established
- applications: Rare disease diagnosis, Cancer mutation profiling, Genetic disorder screening
- development_stage: clinical
- companies: Illumina, Thermo Fisher
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Targeted Gene Panels

- name: Targeted Gene Panels
- category: genomic_diagnostics
- technology_type: sequencing
- mechanism: NGS-based targeted sequencing of selected gene sets
- key_features: High depth coverage, Cost-effective, Fast turnaround, Specific disease focus
- applications: Hereditary cancer screening, Cardiogenomics, Pharmacogenomics
- development_stage: clinical
- companies: Invitae, Myriad Genetics, Foundation Medicine
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Oxford Nanopore Sequencing

- name: Oxford Nanopore Sequencing
- category: genomic_diagnostics
- technology_type: sequencing
- mechanism: Real-time sequencing via protein nanopores detecting DNA strand current changes
- key_features: Real-time results, Long reads (>10kb), Portable (MinION), Direct RNA sequencing
- applications: Field diagnostics, Infectious disease, Structural variant detection, Epigenetic detection
- development_stage: clinical
- companies: Oxford Nanopore Technologies
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Pacific Biosciences HiFi Sequencing

- name: Pacific Biosciences HiFi Sequencing
- category: genomic_diagnostics
- technology_type: sequencing
- mechanism: Single-molecule real-time (SMRT) sequencing with circular consensus
- key_features: High accuracy (>99.9%), Long reads (15-25kb), No amplification bias
- applications: De novo genome assembly, Structural variant detection, Haplotype-resolved sequencing
- development_stage: clinical
- companies: Pacific Biosciences
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Next‐Generation Sequencing (NGS)

- name: Next‐Generation Sequencing (NGS)
- type: Technology
- technology: NGS
- application: Infectious disease diagnosis
- cost: Variable, depends on scale and scope
- status: Established
- description: Next-generation sequencing (NGS) has emerged as a transformative tool for infectious disease diagnosis, offering broad pathogen detection. It allows for the comprehensive identification of pathogens from clinical samples. This technology enables rapid and accurate diagnosis, which is crucial for effective treatment and outbreak management.
- sources: [object Object]

### Rapid Whole Genome Sequencing (rWGS)

- name: Rapid Whole Genome Sequencing (rWGS)
- type: Technology
- technology: WGS
- application: Newborn intensive care and critical diagnosis
- cost: High
- status: Emerging
- description: Rapid whole genome sequencing (rWGS) is a key testing method in genomic medicine that provides quick results for critically ill newborns. It helps in diagnosing genetic conditions rapidly, enabling timely medical interventions. This technology is being expanded in state coverage for improved healthcare outcomes.
- sources: [object Object]

### Pharmacogenomics

- name: Pharmacogenomics
- type: Field
- technology: Genetic testing
- application: Personalized medicine and drug response prediction
- cost: Variable, depends on the test
- status: Established
- description: Pharmacogenomics involves studying how genes affect a person's response to drugs. It helps in predicting which medications will be most effective and which may cause adverse reactions. This field is integral to personalized medicine, optimizing treatment plans based on individual genetic profiles.
- sources: [object Object]

### AI-Powered Liquid Biopsy

- name: AI-Powered Liquid Biopsy
- type: Diagnostic Technology
- technology: AI/ML + ctDNA analysis
- application: Early cancer detection and precision oncology
- cost: Variable
- status: Clinical deployment (2025-2026)
- description: AI-powered liquid biopsy represents the convergence of artificial intelligence and machine learning with circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) analysis for non-invasive cancer detection. This approach overcomes traditional liquid biopsy limitations by using AI algorithms to detect subtle genomic mutation patterns in cell-free DNA at earlier stages than conventional methods. The technology can identify actionable genomic alterations from a single blood draw, enabling real-time tumor profiling and personalized therapy selection. Key developments include SOPHiA GENETICS' partnership with A.D.A.M. Innovations for precision oncology in Japan, and Frontiers-published research demonstrating AI-enhanced ctDNA and tumor-derived exosome detection. The approach significantly improves lung cancer screening precision by identifying genetic mutations before tumors are imaging-visible.
- sources: [object Object], [object Object], [object Object]

### Multi-Modal AI Precision Medicine

- name: Multi-Modal AI Precision Medicine
- type: Platform
- technology: Multi-omics integration + AI
- application: Comprehensive precision medicine across cancer and rare diseases
- cost: Variable
- status: Emerging (2025-2026)
- description: Multi-modal AI precision medicine platforms integrate genomics, medical imaging, electronic health records, and proteomics data using advanced AI models to provide comprehensive diagnostic and treatment recommendations. Unlike single-data-type approaches, these platforms fuse multiple data streams — including whole-genome sequencing, radiology images, pathology slides, and clinical histories — into unified AI models that can detect patterns invisible to individual modalities. Published in Frontiers in Artificial Intelligence (2025), research demonstrates that multi-modal AI enhances liquid biopsy analysis by combining ctDNA data with imaging features, improving early cancer detection rates. The approach is expanding into rare disease diagnosis, chronic condition management, and treatment response prediction. Precision Medicine World Conference (PMWC) 2025 highlighted this as a key trend reshaping genomic care through 2026.
- sources: [object Object], [object Object], [object Object]

### Spatial Transcriptomics

- name: Spatial Transcriptomics
- category: genomic_diagnostics
- technology_type: spatial omics
- mechanism: Maps gene expression patterns to tissue spatial coordinates, preserving geographical information lost in single-cell dissociation. Technologies include 10x Visium, MERFISH, Stereo-seq, and CosMx
- key_features: Resolves gene expression in 2D/3D tissue context, Sub-cellular resolution (Stereo-seq, CosMx), Can profile 1000+ genes simultaneously, Reveals tumor microenvironment architecture
- applications: Tumor microenvironment profiling, Developmental biology spatial atlases, Drug response heterogeneity, Neuroscience brain mapping
- advantages: Spatial context preserved, Multi-gene profiling, Clinical pathology integration
- limitations: High cost per sample, Data analysis complexity, Sample preparation challenges
- companies: 10x Genomics, Vizgen, NanoString, BGI
- development_stage: clinical
- sources: Nature Biotechnology, 10x Genomics, Cell
- confidence: high
- date_added: 2026-06-25

### Long-Read Sequencing (PacBio Revio, Oxford Nanopore)

- name: Long-Read Sequencing (PacBio Revio, Oxford Nanopore)
- category: genomic_diagnostics
- technology_type: sequencing
- mechanism: Single-molecule sequencing generating reads >10kb, resolving repetitive regions, structural variants, and epigenetic modifications that short-read NGS cannot detect
- key_features: Read lengths 10-100kb+ (vs 150-300bp for NGS), Direct detection of DNA methylation without bisulfite, Resolves complex structural variants, Full-length transcript isoform sequencing (Iso-Seq)
- applications: Structural variant detection in genetic diseases, Repeat expansion disorders (Huntington, Fragile X), HLA typing for transplantation, Microbial genome assembly
- advantages: Resolves repeats and SVs, Native methylation detection, Full-length transcripts
- limitations: Higher per-base cost than NGS, Lower throughput (improving), Higher error rate (improving)
- companies: Pacific Biosciences, Oxford Nanopore Technologies
- development_stage: clinical
- sources: Nature Reviews Genetics, PacBio, Oxford Nanopore
- confidence: high
- date_added: 2026-06-25

### AI-Powered Variant Interpretation

- name: AI-Powered Variant Interpretation
- category: genomic_diagnostics
- technology_type: AI/ML diagnostics
- mechanism: Deep learning models (DeepVariant, SEQUIA, AlphaMissense) that classify genomic variants of uncertain significance (VUS) by integrating genomic, transcriptomic, and clinical data
- key_features: DeepVariant: Google's CNN-based variant caller with >99.99% accuracy, AlphaMissense: DeepMind's model predicting pathogenicity of all 71M missense variants, SEQUIA: Ensemble approach integrating multiple AI models, Reduces VUS rate from ~30% to <10% in clinical genomics
- applications: Clinical genomic testing interpretation, Rare disease diagnosis, Pharmacogenomics, Population screening
- advantages: Scalable, Reduces VUS burden, Continuously improving
- limitations: Training data bias, Black-box interpretability, Needs clinical validation
- companies: Google DeepMind, Illumina, Congenica, Invitae
- development_stage: clinical
- sources: Science (AlphaMissense, Nature Biotechnology, Google DeepMind
- confidence: high
- date_added: 2026-06-25

## gene_delivery

### Adeno-Associated Virus (AAV) Vectors

- name: Adeno-Associated Virus (AAV) Vectors
- category: gene_delivery
- delivery_type: viral vector
- mechanism: Non-integrating viral vector for gene delivery
- key_features: Low immunogenicity, Long-term expression, Multiple serotypes for tissue targeting, Non-integrating
- applications: Gene therapy (Luxturna, Zolgensma), In vivo gene delivery, Retinal diseases, Neuromuscular disorders
- development_stage: clinical/commercial
- companies: Spark Therapeutics, Novartis, RegenxBio
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Lipid Nanoparticles (LNPs)

- name: Lipid Nanoparticles (LNPs)
- category: gene_delivery
- delivery_type: non-viral
- mechanism: Lipid-based nanoparticle encapsulation for nucleic acid delivery
- key_features: Low immunogenicity, High delivery efficiency, Scalable manufacturing, Validated by mRNA vaccines
- applications: mRNA vaccine delivery, CRISPR component delivery, siRNA delivery, In vivo gene editing
- development_stage: clinical/commercial
- companies: Moderna, Pfizer, Intellia Therapeutics
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

- mechanism: Engineered LNPs for delivery beyond the liver

- applications: Lung delivery, Brain delivery, Spleen targeting, Tumor targeting

- mechanism: Redesigned LNP components for improved mRNA delivery precision

### Adenoviral Vectors

- name: Adenoviral Vectors
- category: gene_delivery
- delivery_type: viral vector
- mechanism: DNA virus vector for transient gene expression
- key_features: High transduction efficiency, Large cargo capacity, Transient expression, Pre-existing immunity concern
- applications: Vaccines (J&J COVID), Cancer gene therapy, Oncolytic virotherapy
- development_stage: clinical/commercial
- companies: Janssen, AstraZeneca
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Lentiviral Vectors

- name: Lentiviral Vectors
- category: gene_delivery
- delivery_type: viral vector
- mechanism: Integrating retroviral vector for stable gene expression
- key_features: Stable integration, Large cargo capacity (~8kb), Infects dividing and non-dividing cells
- applications: CAR-T cell engineering, Ex vivo gene therapy, Stem cell modification
- development_stage: clinical/commercial
- companies: Kite Pharma, Novartis, Bluebird Bio
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### LNP vs AAV Debate (2026)

- name: LNP vs AAV Debate (2026)
- category: gene_delivery
- delivery_type: comparison
- mechanism: Emerging comparison of LNP and AAV for in vivo gene therapy
- key_features: LNPs gaining ground for in vivo editing, AAV established for gene addition, Redosing possible with LNPs, AAV has durability advantage
- applications: In vivo gene therapy, Gene editing delivery
- development_stage: evolving
- companies: 
- sources: web_search
- confidence: medium
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Non-Viral LNP Diabetes Gene Therapy (Genprex)

- name: Non-Viral LNP Diabetes Gene Therapy (Genprex)
- category: gene_delivery
- delivery_type: non-viral
- mechanism: LNP delivery of genes for diabetes treatment without viral vectors
- key_features: Non-viral approach, Diabetes gene therapy, LNP-mediated delivery
- applications: Type 1 diabetes, Type 2 diabetes, Insulin regulation
- development_stage: preclinical
- companies: Genprex
- sources: web_search
- confidence: medium
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Exosome-Based Delivery

- name: Exosome-Based Delivery
- category: gene_delivery

- mechanism: Natural extracellular vesicles for nucleic acid and protein delivery
- key_features: Natural biocompatibility, Cross biological barriers, Low immunogenicity, BBB penetration potential
- applications: Brain delivery, Cancer therapy, RNA delivery

### LNP for RNA Therapeutics

- name: LNP for RNA Therapeutics
- category: gene_delivery
- delivery_type: non-viral
- mechanism: LNP delivery enabling RNA therapeutic revolution
- key_features: Clinically validated platform, Enables mRNA, siRNA, saRNA delivery, Scalable manufacturing
- applications: mRNA vaccines, RNA therapeutics, Gene editing components

- companies: Moderna, Pfizer, Alnylam

- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### AAV9

- name: AAV9
- type: Viral Vector
- capacity: 4.7 kb
- tropism: Broad, including central nervous system, skeletal muscle, and liver
- advantage: Low immunogenicity, long-term expression, FDA-approved for spinal muscular atrophy
- status: Clinical/Approved
- description: AAV9 is a widely used serotype of adeno-associated virus vectors, known for its ability to transduce various tissues, including the central nervous system. It has been successfully employed in gene therapies like Zolgensma for spinal muscular atrophy. Its minimal pathogenicity and ability to establish long-term transgene expression make it a cornerstone of in vivo gene delivery.
- sources: [object Object], [object Object]

### Lentivirus

- name: Lentivirus
- type: Viral Vector
- capacity: 8-10 kb
- tropism: Broad, can be pseudotyped for specific targeting
- advantage: Large cargo capacity, integrates into host genome for stable expression
- status: Preclinical/Clinical
- description: Lentiviral vectors are derived from retroviruses and are capable of transducing both dividing and non-dividing cells. Their primary advantage is the ability to deliver large genetic payloads and achieve long-term, stable expression through genomic integration. They are commonly used in ex vivo gene therapies, such as CAR-T cell treatments.
- sources: [object Object]

### Adenovirus

- name: Adenovirus
- type: Viral Vector
- capacity: 36 kb
- tropism: Broad, high transduction efficiency in many cell types
- advantage: Very large cargo capacity, high transduction efficiency
- status: Clinical/Approved
- description: Adenoviral vectors are known for their high transduction efficiency and ability to accommodate large genetic inserts, making them suitable for vaccines and gene therapies. However, they can elicit strong immune responses, which may limit their use for repeated administration. They have been successfully used in vaccines, such as those for COVID-19.
- sources: [object Object]

### AAV serotypes

- name: AAV serotypes
- type: Viral Vector
- capacity: 4.7 kb
- tropism: Varies by serotype (e.g., AAV1, AAV2, AAV5, AAV8)
- advantage: Versatile tropism, low immunogenicity, long-term expression
- status: Clinical/Approved
- description: Different AAV serotypes exhibit unique tropisms, allowing for targeted delivery to specific tissues. For example, AAV1 is effective for muscle, while AAV8 has a strong affinity for the liver. This versatility makes AAV a highly adaptable platform for developing gene therapies for a wide range of diseases.
- sources: [object Object], [object Object]

### SORT LNP (Selective Organ Targeting Lipid Nanoparticles)

- name: SORT LNP (Selective Organ Targeting Lipid Nanoparticles)
- category: gene_delivery
- delivery_type: non-viral vector
- mechanism: Engineered LNPs with adjustable surface charge (via SORT molecules) that redirect delivery to specific organs: lungs (positive), spleen (neutral), liver (negative)
- key_features: Tunable organ selectivity by adjusting SORT molecule ratio, Non-viral — avoids anti-capsid immunity, Lower manufacturing cost than AAV vectors, Repeat dosing possible due to low immunogenicity
- applications: Lung-targeted mRNA delivery for cystic fibrosis, Spleen-targeted delivery for immune cell modification, Liver-targeted gene editing for metabolic diseases
- advantages: Tissue-specific targeting, Scalable manufacturing, Redoseable
- limitations: Transient expression, Lower efficiency than viral vectors for some tissues
- companies: ReCode Therapeutics, Generation Bio
- development_stage: clinical
- sources: Nature Materials, PNAS, ReCode Therapeutics
- confidence: high
- date_added: 2026-06-25

### Engineered AAV Capsids (AAV-PHP.eB, AAVrh10)

- name: Engineered AAV Capsids (AAV-PHP.eB, AAVrh10)
- category: gene_delivery
- delivery_type: viral vector
- mechanism: Synthetically evolved AAV capsids with enhanced tropism for specific tissues — AAV-PHP.eB crosses blood-brain barrier, AAVrh10 targets heart and skeletal muscle
- key_features: AAV-PHP.eB: Systemic IV delivery to CNS, crossing BBB in mice, AAVrh10: Cardiac and skeletal muscle tropism, Engineered capsids evade pre-existing anti-AAV antibodies, Created by directed evolution (CREATE platform)
- applications: CNS-targeted gene therapy (AAV-PHP.eB), Cardiac gene therapy for heart failure, Whole-body gene delivery for systemic diseases
- advantages: Tissue-specific tropism, Reduced off-target delivery, Potential for immune evasion
- limitations: PHP.eB efficacy varies between species (mouse vs human), Manufacturing complexity
- companies: Dyno Therapeutics, Voyager Therapeutics, Sarepta
- development_stage: preclinical/clinical
- sources: Nature Neuroscience, Cell, Dyno Therapeutics
- confidence: high
- date_added: 2026-06-25

### Exosome-Mediated Gene Delivery

- name: Exosome-Mediated Gene Delivery
- category: gene_delivery
- delivery_type: non-viral vector
- mechanism: Engineered exosomes loaded with genetic cargo (mRNA, siRNA, DNA) using surface modifications for cell-type-specific targeting
- key_features: Natural biocompatibility — low immunogenicity, Crosses biological barriers including BBB, Can be engineered with targeting ligands, Intrinsic tissue homing properties
- applications: Neurological disease (BBB-crossing exosomes), Cancer-targeted siRNA delivery, mRNA vaccine delivery
- advantages: Natural origin, BBB penetration, Low toxicity, Tissue tropism
- limitations: Loading efficiency challenges, Scalability of production, Heterogeneity of preparations
- companies: Codiak BioSciences, Capricor Therapeutics, Evox Therapeutics
- development_stage: preclinical/clinical
- sources: Nature Nanotechnology, Science Translational Medicine
- confidence: high
- date_added: 2026-06-25

### Integrase-Mediated LNP Delivery (Sleeping Beauty, piggyBac)

- name: Integrase-Mediated LNP Delivery (Sleeping Beauty, piggyBac)
- category: gene_delivery
- delivery_type: non-viral vector
- mechanism: LNP-delivered transposon systems (Sleeping Beauty, piggyBac) enabling stable genomic integration of therapeutic genes without viral vectors
- key_features: Stable integration without viral components, Larger cargo capacity than AAV (>100kb vs ~4.7kb), Lower insertional mutagenesis risk than lentivirus, Cost-effective manufacturing
- applications: CAR-T cell manufacturing (non-viral), Hemophilia B gene therapy (factor IX), In vivo gene integration for monogenic diseases
- advantages: Large cargo capacity, Lower cost, Stable expression, Non-viral
- limitationsations: Integration site less predictable, Lower efficiency than viral in some tissues
- limitations: Integration site less predictable, Lower efficiency than viral in some tissues
- companies: Poseida Therapeutics, Aldevron, MaxCyte
- development_stage: clinical
- sources: Nature Biotechnology, Molecular Therapy
- confidence: high
- date_added: 2026-06-25

## gene_editing_tools

### CRISPR-Cas9

- name: CRISPR-Cas9
- category: gene_editing_tools
- editing_type: knockout/knock-in
- mechanism: RNA-guided double-strand break via Cas9 nuclease
- key_features: PAM: NGG, High efficiency, Widely validated
- applications: Gene knockout, Gene knock-in, Transcriptional regulation
- development_stage: mature
- companies: CRISPR Therapeutics, Editas Medicine, Intellia Therapeutics
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### CRISPR-Cas12a (Cpf1)

- name: CRISPR-Cas12a (Cpf1)
- category: gene_editing_tools
- editing_type: knockout
- mechanism: RNA-guided DSB with staggered cuts via Cas12a
- key_features: PAM: TTTV, Staggered cut, Smaller guide RNA
- applications: Multiplex editing, Diagnostics (DETECTR), Gene knockout
- development_stage: mature
- companies: Broad Institute, Mammoth Biosciences
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### CRISPR-Cas13

- name: CRISPR-Cas13
- category: gene_editing_tools
- editing_type: RNA targeting
- mechanism: RNA-guided RNA cleavage via Cas13
- key_features: Targets RNA not DNA, Collateral cleavage activity, No permanent genome changes
- applications: RNA knockdown, Diagnostics (SHERLOCK), Transient gene regulation
- development_stage: mature
- companies: Sherlock Biosciences, Mammoth Biosciences
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Cytosine Base Editor (CBE)

- name: Cytosine Base Editor (CBE)
- category: gene_editing_tools
- editing_type: base editing
- mechanism: Deaminase-fused Cas9 nickase converts C→T (G→A)
- key_features: No double-strand breaks, Single nucleotide precision, C→T conversion
- applications: Point mutation correction, Stop codon introduction, Disease modeling
- development_stage: clinical
- companies: Beam Therapeutics, Pairwise
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Adenine Base Editor (ABE)

- name: Adenine Base Editor (ABE)
- category: gene_editing_tools
- editing_type: base editing
- mechanism: Deaminase-fused Cas9 nickase converts A→G (T→C)
- key_features: No double-strand breaks, A→G conversion, Complementary to CBE
- applications: Point mutation correction, Sickle cell disease, Genetic disease therapy
- development_stage: clinical
- companies: Beam Therapeutics, Verve Therapeutics
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Prime Editor (PE)

- name: Prime Editor (PE)
- category: gene_editing_tools
- editing_type: prime editing
- mechanism: Cas9 nickase-reverse transcriptase fusion using pegRNA
- key_features: Search-and-replace, All 12 point mutations, Small insertions/deletions, No DSB required
- applications: Precise gene correction, Insertions up to 50bp, Complex edits
- development_stage: clinical
- companies: Prime Medicine
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### CRISPR Epigenome Editors

- name: CRISPR Epigenome Editors
- category: gene_editing_tools
- editing_type: epigenetic modification
- mechanism: Catalytically dead Cas9 (dCas9) fused to epigenetic modifiers
- key_features: No DNA sequence change, Reversible modifications, CRISPRa/CRISPRi

### CRISPR Epigenome Editor (MEK)

- name: CRISPR Epigenome Editor (MEK)
- category: gene_editing_tools
- editing_type: epigenetic activation
- mechanism: dCas9 fused to transcriptional activators removes repressive methylation marks (H3K9me3) to durably reactivate silenced genes without DNA cutting
- key_features: No double-strand breaks, Durable gene reactivation, Multiplexed gene control, Transient delivery achieves sustained effect
- applications: Reactivating silenced tumor suppressor genes, Gene therapy for genetic silencing disorders, Epigenetic disease modification
- development_stage: preclinical
- companies: Tune Therapeutics, Chroma Medicine
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-06-07T22:08:08.574Z
- last_updated: 2026-06-07T22:08:08.574Z

### CRISPRoff v2

- name: CRISPRoff v2
- category: gene_editing_tools
- editing_type: epigenetic silencing
- mechanism: Enhanced dCas9-DNMT3A-DNMT3L fusion for programmable and heritable DNA methylation-based gene silencing
- key_features: Heritable gene silencing through cell division, No DNA sequence modification, Improved efficiency over original CRISPRoff, RNA-guided targeting
- applications: Durable gene knockdown for therapy, Epigenetic therapy for cancers, Stem cell differentiation control
- development_stage: preclinical
- companies: Vertex Pharmaceuticals
- sources: web_search

### Fast-Control CRISPR-Cas9 Protein System

- name: Fast-Control CRISPR-Cas9 Protein System
- category: gene_editing_tools
- editing_type: precision editing with reduced off-target
- mechanism: Cell-permeable protein system that rapidly controls CRISPR-Cas9 activity, reducing off-target effects through fast-acting regulation
- key_features: Fast-acting protein delivery, Cell-permeable system, Reduced off-target editing, Temporal control of Cas9 activity
- applications: Precision gene therapy, Reduced off-target clinical applications, Therapeutic genome editing safety improvement
- development_stage: preclinical
- companies: 
- sources: web_search
- confidence: medium
- first_seen: 2026-06-07T22:08:08.574Z
- last_updated: 2026-06-07T22:08:08.574Z

### CRISPR-Cas12a

- name: CRISPR-Cas12a
- type: Gene editing tool
- mechanism: Similar to Cas9, Cas12a uses a guide RNA to target specific DNA sequences and creates a double-strand break, but it has different PAM requirements and can process its own guide RNA.
- target: Specific DNA sequences for genetic modifications, enabling simultaneous assessment of multiple genetic changes.
- advantage: Allows for more seamless gene editing and improved disease modeling by enabling multiplexed edits.
- status: Emerging tool with applications in research and disease modeling.
- description: CRISPR-Cas12a is a new tool that helps researchers simultaneously assess the impact of multiple genetic changes involved in diseases. It offers a more seamless approach to gene editing compared to traditional Cas9. This tool is particularly useful for creating improved disease models and studying complex genetic interactions.
- sources: [object Object]

### Base Editors

- name: Base Editors
- type: Gene editing tool
- mechanism: Derived from the CRISPR-Cas system, base editors fuse a catalytically impaired Cas protein with a base-modifying enzyme to directly convert one base pair to another without requiring double-strand breaks.
- target: Specific point mutations in DNA to correct disease-causing single nucleotide variants.
- advantage: Enables precise point mutations without inducing double-strand breaks, reducing the risk of unwanted genomic alterations.
- status: Advanced technology with clinical applications, recognized by the 2025 Breakthrough Prize.
- description: Base editors are a novel genome editing technology developed to introduce specific point mutations without requiring DNA double-strand breaks. They are particularly useful for correcting single nucleotide variants that cause genetic diseases. The technology was recognized with the 2025 Breakthrough Prize in Life Sciences for its innovative approach to precision genome editing.
- sources: [object Object], [object Object]

### Prime Editors

- name: Prime Editors
- type: Gene editing tool
- mechanism: Prime editing uses a Cas9 nickase fused to a reverse transcriptase and a prime editing guide RNA (pegRNA) to directly write new genetic information into a specified DNA site without requiring double-strand breaks or donor templates.
- target: Precise genetic modifications, including insertions, deletions, and all base-to-base conversions.
- advantage: Enables precise genome editing without double-strand breaks, expanding the scope and capabilities of genetic modifications.
- status: Advanced technology with ongoing improvements for efficiency and broader applications.
- description: Prime editing is an advanced genome editing technology that can replace disease-causing sections of DNA with new segments without inducing double-strand breaks. It substantially expands the scope of genome editing and can correct the vast majority of known genetic variants. The technology is continuously being improved to enhance efficiency and expand its potential therapeutic applications.
- sources: [object Object], [object Object], [object Object]

- headquarters: Cambridge, MA, USA

The company went public in 2026 with a $400 million IPO, marking the largest biotech offering of the year. It aims to revolutionize drug discovery by using generative models to create new medicines for a range of diseases.

### Verve Therapeutics

- name: Verve Therapeutics
- category: biotech_companies
- ticker: VERV
- focus: Cardiovascular gene editing — base editing for heart disease
- key_products: VERVE-102 — base editing of PCSK9 for hypercholesterolemia, VERVE-201 — base editing of ANGPTL3 for cardiovascular disease

- founded: 2018
- development_stage: clinical

- date_added: 2026-06-25

- key_products: CRG-022 — CD22 CAR-T for relapsed/refractory B-cell malignancies, CRG-023 — dual-targeting CD19/CD22 CAR-T

- sources: Cargo Therapeutics, SEC filings, ASH
- confidence: high
- date_added: 2026-06-25

### Tessera Therapeutics

- name: Tessera Therapeutics
- category: biotech_companies
- ticker: private
- focus: Gene Writing — mobile genetic element-based programmable gene insertion
- key_products: Gene Writing platform — RNA-templated and DNA-templated gene writing, Preclinical programs in metabolic disease and hematology
- hq: Cambridge, MA, USA
- founded: 2020
- development_stage: preclinical
- key_achievements: Raised $300M+ in Series B funding (2022), valuing company at >$1B, Developed Gene Writing technology based on mobile genetic elements (MGEs), Can write entire genes kilobases in length without double-strand breaks, Published proof-of-concept in Nature Biotechnology (2024), Potential to overcome key limitations of CRISPR for large gene insertion
- sources: Tessera Therapeutics, Nature Biotechnology, STAT News
- confidence: high
- date_added: 2026-06-25

### Metagenomi

- name: Metagenomi
- category: biotech_companies
- ticker: MGX
- focus: Discovering novel gene editing systems from metagenomic mining
- key_products: MGX-001 — programmable nuclease for in vivo gene editing, MGX-002 — base editor platform, Meta Novo platform — AI-driven metagenomic discovery
- hq: Emeryville, CA, USA
- founded: 2016
- development_stage: preclinical
- key_achievements: IPO January 2024, raising ~$94M, Discovered novel CRISPR-Cas systems, recombinases, and integrases from environmental metagenomes, Partnership with Moderna for in vivo gene editing therapeutics, Pipeline includes programs for cardiovascular, metabolic, and neurological diseases
- sources: Metagenomi, SEC filings, Nature
- confidence: high
- date_added: 2026-06-25

## regenerative_medicine

### Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells (iPSCs)

- name: Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells (iPSCs)
- category: regenerative_medicine
- technology_type: stem cell
- mechanism: Reprogramming adult cells to pluripotent state via Yamanaka factors
- key_features: Patient-specific cells, Avoids ethical concerns of ESCs, Unlimited self-renewal, Differentiation to any cell type
- applications: Disease modeling, Drug screening, Cell therapy, Personalized medicine
- development_stage: clinical
- companies: Fujifilm Cellular Dynamics, Allele Biotech, CDI Biosciences
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Mesenchymal Stem Cells (MSCs)

- name: Mesenchymal Stem Cells (MSCs)
- category: regenerative_medicine
- technology_type: stem cell
- mechanism: Adult stem cells with immunomodulatory and regenerative properties
- key_features: Immunomodulatory, Paracrine signaling, Multiple tissue sources, Low immunogenicity
- applications: Orthopedic repair, Autoimmune disease, Graft-versus-host disease, Inflammatory conditions
- development_stage: clinical
- companies: Mesoblast, Pluristem
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### MSC-Derived Exosomes

- name: MSC-Derived Exosomes
- category: regenerative_medicine
- technology_type: cell-free therapy
- mechanism: Extracellular vesicles from MSCs carrying therapeutic cargo
- key_features: Cell-free approach, Paracrine mechanism, Lower safety concerns than cells, Scalable production
- applications: Tissue repair, Anti-inflammatory therapy, Wound healing, Neuroprotection
- development_stage: preclinical/clinical
- companies: 
- sources: web_search
- confidence: medium
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Organoids

- name: Organoids
- category: regenerative_medicine
- technology_type: tissue engineering
- mechanism: 3D self-organizing tissue cultures from stem cells mimicking organ structure
- key_features: 3D organ-like structure, Patient-specific, Drug testing platform, Disease modeling
- applications: Drug discovery, Disease modeling, Personalized therapy testing, Developmental biology
- development_stage: research/preclinical
- companies: HUB Organoids, Emulate
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

- applications: Alzheimer's research, Autism modeling, Neurodevelopmental studies, Drug testing

### Tissue Engineering

- name: Tissue Engineering
- category: regenerative_medicine
- technology_type: tissue engineering
- mechanism: Combining cells, biomaterials, and growth factors to create functional tissues
- key_features: Biomaterial scaffolds, Growth factor delivery, Bioprinting integration, Functional tissue creation
- applications: Skin grafts, Cartilage repair, Bone regeneration, Blood vessel engineering
- development_stage: clinical
- companies: Organogenesis, Humacyte
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### 3D Bioprinting

- name: 3D Bioprinting
- category: regenerative_medicine
- technology_type: manufacturing
- mechanism: Additive manufacturing of living tissues using bioinks containing cells
- key_features: Layer-by-layer deposition, Complex tissue architecture, Multiple cell types, Customizable shapes
- applications: Organ fabrication, Tissue patches, Drug testing models, Implant creation
- development_stage: preclinical
- companies: CELLINK, Organovo, Aspect Biosystems
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Xenotransplantation

- name: Xenotransplantation
- category: regenerative_medicine
- technology_type: transplantation
- mechanism: Transplantation of organs from gene-edited animals to humans
- key_features: Gene-edited pig organs, Addresses organ shortage, CRISPR-enabled modifications
- applications: Kidney xenotransplant, Heart xenotransplant, Liver xenotransplant
- development_stage: clinical (compassionate use)
- companies: eGenesis, Makana Therapeutics, Revivicor
- sources: web_search
- confidence: high
- first_seen: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- last_updated: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

### Biomaterial Scaffolds

- name: Biomaterial Scaffolds

- technology_type: biomaterial
- mechanism: Biocompatible structures providing 3D framework for cell growth and tissue formation
- key_features: Biodegradable, Porosity control, Mechanical properties tunable, Growth factor loading
- applications: Bone regeneration, Cartilage repair, Neural tissue engineering, Cardiac patches
